topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment had become a thick, suffocating blanket. Every creak of the floorboards, every distant siren, seemed amplified, pressing against my skin. I kept catching myself staring at Leo, who was hunched over his laptop in the corner, the blue light illuminating the sharp angles of his concentration. Tonight, however, the usual focused intensity felt twisted, predatory. I noticed the way his fingers moved—not typing casually, but with a deliberate, almost reverent slowness. A strange knot tightened in my chest, a cold, hard pressure that made my breath shallow. Then I saw it: a document open, a title scrawled in elegant script that read, "The Cartography of Elara." My stomach lurched. I stood up, the movement jarring, and walked towards him, my footsteps unnaturally loud on the hardwood. "Leo," I managed, my voice sounding thin and reedy. He flinched, startled, and quickly minimized the screen, a flicker of something guarded crossing his face before he masked it. The air between us crackled with unspoken tension, heavy and charged.

[story 2]

I found myself circling the living room like a trapped animal. My hands were damp, clammy, and I kept running them through my hair, a nervous tic I hadn't indulged in since I was a child. It was the scent of old paper and strong coffee that first alerted me—a scent that now seemed tainted by something secretive. I crept towards the study, drawn by an inexplicable compulsion. The glow from the desk lamp painted shadows across Leo’s profile. He was immersed, his brow furrowed in deep concentration, his jaw tight. I leaned against the doorframe, my gaze fixed on the screen where I could just make out the flow of prose. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped me. It wasn't just writing; it was a portrait, meticulously detailed, every shared glance, every whispered argument cataloged into narrative structure. My pulse hammered a frantic rhythm against my ribs. I swallowed hard, my throat suddenly dry, and the silence of the room felt like a vacuum waiting to implode.

[story 3]

The evening descended into a slow-motion nightmare of internal monitoring. I kept checking the time, the ticking of the clock mocking the frantic energy buzzing beneath my skin. Leo was in the bedroom, ostensibly reading, but I could sense the vigilance radiating off him, a taut wire stretched just before snapping. I watched his posture—rigid, defensive. It was the way he avoided direct eye contact, the subtle clenching of his hands resting on the duvet. Then, I overheard a fragment of conversation, his voice low and almost hypnotic, describing a scene involving my favorite rainy afternoon. A wave of dizzying disorientation washed over me. I moved towards the hallway, my steps hesitant, like wading through deep water. When I finally reached the doorway, I didn't knock. "What are you doing?" The question was barely a whisper, yet it carried the weight of everything I felt—the sudden, terrifying realization that the person I loved was actively crafting a life story centered entirely around me. His eyes snapped up, wide and startled, the practiced calm instantly dissolving into something raw and exposed.
